Self-Attention Gazetteer Embeddings for Named-Entity Recognition

Stanislav Peshterliev, Christophe Dupuy, Imre Kiss

Amazon, Alexa Machine Learning, USA { stanislp,dupuychr,ikiss } @amazon.

125a8637-4669-4031-97f2-1769e1cbe6cb.label: text_chunk
125a8637-4669-4031-97f2-1769e1cbe6cb.text:
com

Abstract

Recent attempts to ingest external knowledge into neural models for named-entity recogni- tion (NER) have exhibited mixed results. In this work, we present GazSelfAttn, a novel gazetteer embedding approach that uses self- attention and match span encoding to build enhanced gazetteer embeddings. In addition, we demonstrate how to build gazetteer re- sources from the open source Wikidata knowl- edge base.

f6e3d4a9-368b-4d28-9159-b0052e8c8024.label: text_chunk
f6e3d4a9-368b-4d28-9159-b0052e8c8024.text:
Evaluations on CoNLL-03 and Ontonotes 5 datasets, show F 1 improvements over baseline model from 92.34 to 92.86 and 89.11 to 89.32 respectively, achieving per- formance comparable to large state-of-the-art models.

1 Introduction

Named-entity recognition (NER) is the task of tagging relevant entities such as person, location and organization in unstructured text.

4c5e895a-a3c3-4d06-822b-76fdcafc0390.label: text_chunk
4c5e895a-a3c3-4d06-822b-76fdcafc0390.text: Modern NER has been dominated by neural models ( Lam- ple et al. , 2016 ; Ma and Hovy , 2016 ) combined with contextual embeddings from language mod- els (LMs) ( Peters et al. , 2018 ; Devlin et al. , 2018 ; Akbik et al. , 2019 ).

855f198a-7d55-4c32-83df-ade841b30abb.label: text_chunk
855f198a-7d55-4c32-83df-ade841b30abb.text: The LMs are pre-trained on large amounts of unlabeled text which allows the NER model to use the syntactic and semantic in- formation captured by the LM embeddings. On the popular benchmark datasets CoNLL-03 ( Sang and De Meulder , 2003 ) and Ontonotes 5 ( Weischedel et al.

880f2e4e-684d-47ee-9705-f1e5d5609f8e.label: text_chunk
880f2e4e-684d-47ee-9705-f1e5d5609f8e.text: , 2013 ), neural models with LMs achieved state-of-the-art results without gazetteers features, unlike earlier approaches that heavily relied on them ( Florian et al. , 2003 ). Gazetteers are lists that contain entities such as cities, countries, and per- son names. The gazetteers are matched against un- structured text to provide additional features to the model. Data for building gazetteers is available for multiple language from structured data resources

c11dffe0-b2c3-4056-aab3-5855eeef8516.label: text_chunk
c11dffe0-b2c3-4056-aab3-5855eeef8516.text: such as Wikipedia, DBpedia ( Auer et al. , 2007 ) and Wikidata ( Vrandeˇci´c and Kr¨otzsch , 2014 ).In this paper, we propose GazSelfAttn, a novel gazetteer embedding approach that uses self- attention and match span encoding to build en- hanced gazetteer representation.

8999770d-7dc3-4193-a802-1ea9832128ee.label: text_chunk
8999770d-7dc3-4193-a802-1ea9832128ee.text: GazSelfAttn em- beddings are concatenated with the input to a LSTM ( Hochreiter and Schmidhuber , 1997 ) or CNN ( Strubell et al. , 2017 ) sequence layer and are trained end-to-end with the model.

fdca53a5-d566-4c15-afee-aa6133244ea8.label: text_chunk
fdca53a5-d566-4c15-afee-aa6133244ea8.text:
In addition, we show how to extract general gazetteers from the Wikidata, a structured knowledge-base which is part of the Wikipedia project.Our contributions are the following:

• We propose novel gazetteer embeddings that use self-attention combined with match span encoding.

• We enhance gazetteer matching with multi- token and single-token matches in the same representation.

• We demonstrate how to use Wikidata with entity popularity filtering as a resource for building gazetteers.

a813b476-2e29-4f0a-8eec-3c0bbc993a27.label: text_chunk
a813b476-2e29-4f0a-8eec-3c0bbc993a27.text:
GazSelfAttn evaluations on CoNLL-03 and Ontonotes 5 datasets show F 1 score improvement over baseline model from 92.34 to 92.86 and from 89.11 to 89.32 respectively. Moreover, we perform ablation experiments to study the contribution of the different model components.

2 Related Work

Recently, researchers added gazetteers to neural sequence models. Magnolini et al.

f513c5a0-25de-46e8-bee5-dff79fb08e56.label: text_chunk
f513c5a0-25de-46e8-bee5-dff79fb08e56.text:
( 2019 ) demon- strated small improvements on large datasets and bigger improvements on small datasets. Lin et al. ( 2019 ) proposed to train a gazetteer attentive net- work to learn name regularities and spans of NER entities. Liu et al. ( 2019 ) demonstrated that trained gazetteers scoring models combined with hybrid


semi-Markov conditional random field (HSCRF) layer improve overall performance.

b82f1fde-8092-420d-9f03-4a9722193453.label: text_chunk
b82f1fde-8092-420d-9f03-4a9722193453.text: The HSCRF layer predicts a set of candidate spans that are rescored using a gazetteer classifier model. The HSCRF approach differs from the common ap- proach of including gazetteers as an embedding in the model. Unlike the work of Liu et al.

a01f51f1-581b-4a87-abcd-50756a544ae0.label: text_chunk
a01f51f1-581b-4a87-abcd-50756a544ae0.text: ( 2019 ), our GazSelfAttn does not require training a sepa- rate gazetteer classifier and the HSCRF layer, thus our approach works with any standard output layer such as conditional random field (CRF) ( Lafferty et al. , 2001 ).Wu et al. ( 2018 ) proposed an auto-encoding loss with hand-crafted features, including gazetteers, to improve accuracy.

4f1cd4fe-d6cc-4e25-83e7-dbbabdc7699e.label: text_chunk
4f1cd4fe-d6cc-4e25-83e7-dbbabdc7699e.text: However, they did not find that gazetteer features significantly improve accuracy.Extracting gazetteers from structure knowl- edge sources was investigated by Torisawa et al. ( 2007 ) and Ratinov and Roth ( 2009 ). They used Wikipedia’s instance of relationship as a resource for building gazetteers with classical machine learn- ing models. Compared to Wikidata, the data ex- tracted from Wikipedia is smaller and noisier.

4edff6b1-2e16-44c8-ae41-103b73548e62.label: text_chunk
4edff6b1-2e16-44c8-ae41-103b73548e62.text:
Similar to this paper, Song et al. ( 2020 ) used Wikidata as a gazetteer resource. However, they did not use entity popularity to filter ambiguous entities and their gazetteer model features use simple one- hot encoding.

3 Approach

3.1 Model Architecture

We add GazSelfAttn embeddings to the popu- lar Neural CRF model architecture with ELMo LM embeddings from Peters et al. ( 2018 ).

ccd79b6c-e662-439f-aa02-1a4233f2ecc4.label: text_chunk
ccd79b6c-e662-439f-aa02-1a4233f2ecc4.text:
Fig- ure 1 depicts the model, which consists of Glove word embeddings ( Pennington et al.

 , 2014 ), Char- CNN ( Chiu and Nichols , 2016 ; Ma and Hovy , 2016 ), ELMo embeddings, Bi-LSTM, and output CRF layer with BILOU (Beginning Inside Last Out- side Unit) labels encoding ( Konkol and Konop´ık , 2015 ).

3cdb8971-0fe4-40c3-ba50-3babf10d5892.label: text_chunk
3cdb8971-0fe4-40c3-ba50-3babf10d5892.text:
Note that, we concatenate the gazetteer embeddings to the Bi-LSTM input.

3.2 Gazetteers

In this section, we address the issue of building a high-quality gazetteer dictionary M that maps entities to types, e.g., M [Andy Murray] → Per- son. In this work, we use Wikidata, an open source structured knowledge-base, as the source of gazetteers.

70c8589f-4dbb-4d76-9b40-9d92ffc5331d.label: text_chunk
70c8589f-4dbb-4d76-9b40-9d92ffc5331d.text:
Although, Wikidata and DBpedia are similar knowledge bases, we choose Wikidata
Figure 1: Model architecture with gazetteer embed- dings.

because, as of 2019, it provides data on around 45 million entities compared to around 5 million in DBpedia.Wikidata is organized as entities and properties. Entities can be concrete (Boston, NATO, Michael Jordan) and abstract (City, Organization, Person). Properties describe an entity relations.

58adf2f8-c4f5-4ca8-b6c0-4200c764ea3b.label: text_chunk
58adf2f8-c4f5-4ca8-b6c0-4200c764ea3b.text: For exam- ple, Boston instance of City and Boston part of Massachusetts; both instance of and part of are properties.

8f302083-a92e-4bf6-a194-e38048880767.label: text_chunk
8f302083-a92e-4bf6-a194-e38048880767.text: Also, each entity is associated with sitelink count 1 which tacks mentions of the entity on Wikimedia website and can be used as proxy for popularity.To extract gazetteers from Wikidata, we pro- cess the official dumps 2 into tuples of entity and type based only on the left and right part of the instance of triplet, example resulting tuples are Boston → City and Massachusetts → State.

50dcf768-49ff-4dff-9c76-6014b2b24a61.label: text_chunk
50dcf768-49ff-4dff-9c76-6014b2b24a61.text: Each entity is associated with a set of aliases, we keep only the aliases that are less than seven tokens long. Example aliases for Boston are “Beantown” and “The Cradle of Liberty”. If there are multiple types per alias, we use the sitelink count to keep the six most popular types. The sitelink filtering is impor- tant to reduce the infrequent meanings of an entity in the gazetteer data.

8d1646db-f208-46cf-8efe-592aceabc682.label: text_chunk
8d1646db-f208-46cf-8efe-592aceabc682.text:
The Wikidata types that we obtain after process- ing the Wikidata dumps are fine-grained. However, certain NER tasks require coarse-grained types.1 https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Help: Sitelinks 2 dumps.wikimedia.org/wikidatawiki
For instance, CoNLL-03 task has a single Loca- tion label that consists of cities, states, countries, and other geographic location.

8cf0adaa-f38e-4d5f-822e-87a0fd570e38.label: text_chunk
8cf0adaa-f38e-4d5f-822e-87a0fd570e38.text: To move from fine-grained to coarse-grained types, we use the Wikidata hierarchical structure induced by the sub- class of property. Examples of subclass of hierar- chies in Wikidata are: City → Human Settlement → Geographic Location, and Artist → Creator → Person. We change the types granularity depend- ing on the NER task by traversing up, from fine- grained types to the target coarse-grained types.

03cf0f00-c90b-41f0-8a14-f0244e31e002.label: text_chunk
03cf0f00-c90b-41f0-8a14-f0244e31e002.text:
For instance, we merge the Artist and Painter types to Person, and the River and Mountain types to Location.

3.3 Gazetteer Matching

Gazetteer matching is the process of assigning gazetteer features to sentence tokens.

5fe8c2cc-f4f4-48de-9a94-0cb9ac58a654.label: text_chunk
5fe8c2cc-f4f4-48de-9a94-0cb9ac58a654.text:
Formally, given a gazetteer dictionary M that maps entities to types, and a sentence S = ( t 1 , t 2 , ., t n ) with tokens t i , we have to find the m gazetteer types { g 1 i , g 2 i , ., g m i } and spans { s 1 i , s 2 i , .

, s m i } for every token t i .

e3e30a2b-0e92-4c2a-922e-16351e80a62d.label: text_chunk
e3e30a2b-0e92-4c2a-922e-16351e80a62d.text: The set notation {} indicates that multi- ple m matches are allowed per token. The match span { s j i } represents positional information which encodes multi-token matches. The match spans are encoded using a BILU (Beginning Inside Last Unit) tags, similar to the BILOU tags that we use to encode the NER labels.In general, there are two methods for gazetteer matching: multi-token and single-token.

0714b729-02fd-4026-bb8e-6273bceac65c.label: text_chunk
0714b729-02fd-4026-bb8e-6273bceac65c.text: Multi- token matching is searching for the longest seg- ments of the sentence that are in M . For instance, given M [New York] → State, M [New York City] → City and the sentence “Yesterday in New York City”, the multi-token matcher assigns the City gazetteer type to the longest segment “New York City”. Single-token matching is searching to match any vocabulary word from a gazetteer type.

bc523b88-90ef-47f4-a56d-af3fde954ae0.label: text_chunk
bc523b88-90ef-47f4-a56d-af3fde954ae0.text: In the earlier example, each word from the sentence is in- dividually matched to the tokens in M , thus “New” and “York” are individually matched to both City and State, and “City” is matched only to City.Magnolini et al. ( 2019 ) research shows that both multi-token and single-token matching perform bet- ter on certain datasets.

ab7baa3a-21fe-4c0e-be01-1e189b38c5fa.label: text_chunk
ab7baa3a-21fe-4c0e-be01-1e189b38c5fa.text:
We propose to combine both methods: we tag the multi-token matches with BILU tags, and the single-token matches with a Sin- gle (S) tag. The single-token matches are used only if multi-token matches are not present. We con- sider that the single-token matches are high-recall
low-precision, and multi-token matches are low- recall and high-precision. Thus, a combination of both works better than individually.

c2dba13d-2aa8-4449-9f32-6b44b511189b.label: text_chunk
c2dba13d-2aa8-4449-9f32-6b44b511189b.text: Example sen- tences are: “Yesterday in New(City-B) York(City- I) City(City-L)”, and “Yesterday in York(City-S) City(City-S)” York City is marked with singles tag since M does not have entities for “York City”, “York”, and “City”.Note that gazetteer matching is unsupervised, i.e., we do not have a ground truth of correctly matched sentences for M .

84a6750c-f416-41f0-9745-87b9be497f7e.label: text_chunk
84a6750c-f416-41f0-9745-87b9be497f7e.text:
Furthermore, it is a hard task because of the many variations in writing and ambiguity of entities.

3.

67a10e2b-af03-41e6-a82b-8b467a627f73.text:
4 Gazetteer Embeddings

E i = G [ { g m i } ] ⊕ S [ { s m i } ] (1)
A i = softmax ( E i E T i √ d ) E i (2)
H i = GELU ( WA i + b ) (3)
g i = maxpooling ( H i ) (4)
Equations 1 - 4 shows the gazetteer embedding g i computation for a token t i .

aa7be4f6-a907-4682-8ee2-b717d91ebfee.label: text_chunk
aa7be4f6-a907-4682-8ee2-b717d91ebfee.text:
To compute g i , given a set of m gazetteer types { g m i } and spans { s m i } , we execute the following procedure:

• Equation 1 .

 We embed the sets { g m i } and { s m i } using the embedding matrices G and S . Then, we do an element-wise addition, denoted ⊕ , of the corresponding types and spans embeddings to get a matrix E i .

• Equation 2 .

11f1ef59-c748-4255-9834-b81fd4ca4070.label: text_chunk
11f1ef59-c748-4255-9834-b81fd4ca4070.text:
We compute A i using scaled dot- product self-attention ( Vaswani et al. , 2017 ), where d is the dimensionality of the gazetteer embeddings. The attention contextualizes the embeddings with multiple gazetteer matches per token t i .

• Equation 3 . To add model flexibility,

 we com- pute H i with a position-wise feed-forward layer and GELU activation ( Hendrycks and Gimpel , 2016 ).

5406de3a-af2a-480f-bfe5-896537791bbc.label: text_chunk
5406de3a-af2a-480f-bfe5-896537791bbc.text:
• Equation 4 . Finally,

 we perform max pooling across the embeddings H i to obtain the final gazetteer embedding g i .

3.5 Gazetteer Dropout

To prevent the neural NER model from overfitting on the gazetteers, we use gazetteers dropout ( Yang et al. , 2016 ).

f5e7b2cb-7206-4c74-9ba2-8b763499001a.label: text_chunk
f5e7b2cb-7206-4c74-9ba2-8b763499001a.text:
We randomly set to zero gazetteer em- beddings g i , so the gazetteer vectors that are input to the LSTM become zero. We tune the gazetteer dropout hyperparameter on the validation set.

d3259f49-1450-415c-993c-5c8daa1f4c62.text: , 2014 ), 128 dim Char- CNN ( Chiu and Nichols , 2016 ; Ma and Hovy , 2016 ) embeddings with filter size of 3 and ran- domly initialized 16 dim char embeddings, 1024 pre-trained ELMo pre-trained embeddings, two layer 200 dim Bi-LSTM, and output CRF layer with BILOU (Beginning Inside Last Outside Unit) spans ( Konkol and Konop´ık , 2015 ).

4.3 Ablation study

Table 3 shows ablation experiments.

975e75d2-ae3c-4f4b-9df5-0d024e40bc7a.label: text_chunk
975e75d2-ae3c-4f4b-9df5-0d024e40bc7a.text: We remove components of the gazetteer embedding model from the Neural CRF model. In each experiment, we removed only the specified component. Abla- tions show decreased F 1 score on the development and test set if any of the components is removed. The highest degradation is when single matches are removed which underscores the importance of the combining the gazetteer matching techniques for NER.

e3d25c48-bc80-497f-846a-d9aee748c660.label: text_chunk
e3d25c48-bc80-497f-846a-d9aee748c660.text: We observe that match span encoding is more important for the CoNLL-02 compared to Ononotes 5 because the former has more entities with multiple tokens. Removing the self-attention shows that self-attention is effective at combining information form multiple gazetteers.In addition, we tried moving the gazetteer em- beddings to the CRF layer and using the LSTM token embeddings as attention keys but the F 1 de- graded significantly.

ab7c17be-63c4-4e69-87cb-ac14e3b2220f.label: text_chunk
ab7c17be-63c4-4e69-87cb-ac14e3b2220f.text:
We experimented with adding auto-encoding loss similar to Wu et al. ( 2018 ) and multi-head self-attention. However, we did not ob- serve F 1 score improvements and sometimes small degradations.

5 Conclusion

We presented GazSelfAttn, a novel approach for gazetteer embeddings that uses self-attention and match span positions.

e104ac48-63da-47c4-9cb0-47cb37d52e79.label: text_chunk
e104ac48-63da-47c4-9cb0-47cb37d52e79.text:
Evaluation results of Gaz- SelfAttn show improvement compared to competi- tive baselines and state-of-the-art models on multi- ple datasets.For future work we would like to evaluate Gaz- SelfAttn on non-English language datasets and improve the multi-token gazetteer matching with fuzzy string matching.

 Also, we would like to explore transfer learning of gazetteer embeddings from high-resource to low-resource setting.
